segments.s594-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی این ('this information/news') is the noun phrase that functions as the subject of رسید. به ایران رسید is a verb phrase meaning 'reached Iran' where به marks the goal ('to/into Iran') and رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s594-g10: جوانوی is a nominative noun ('the young one' / 'a youth'). نزد دلیران is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the brave ones' (نزد + indirect object), and رسید is again the past tense 'arrived/appeared'. The clause places the youth at the brave men's assembly.
segments.s594-g11: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones / elders'. ازان کار is a causal phrase 'from that affair / because of that deed' (ازان = 'from/for that', کار = 'affair/deed'). غمگین شدند is the predicate 'became sad' with شدند as the past plural of شدن.
segments.s594-g12: This phrase names the place or person they turned to: بر here marks motion/toward ('to/onto') and is followed by what appears to be a compound proper name or title 'آذر پاک برزین' (Azarpak Barzin). شدند is the past plural 'they went/they became' used here as 'they came/approached' that person/place.
segments.s594-g13: ز یزدان همی خواستند is 'they asked/from God they desired' with ز marking the source ('from' = they begged/implored from God). همی is a poetic/emphatic marker that gives ongoing or earnest sense to خواستند (they wished/asked). آنک رزم introduces the content of their wish: 'that the battle/strife...'.
segments.s594-g14: مگر here introduces the wished-for outcome (poetic usage 'that / so that'). باز گردد is a present-subjunctive sense 'may it return again' (گردد is subjunctive/optative). به شادی و بزم is the goal phrase 'to joy and feasting', with به marking direction/tendency.
segments.s594-g15: چو is the temporal 'when'. منذر is the personal name 'Mundhir' as subject. به نزدیک جهرم رسید is a locative-verb phrase: به نزدیک = 'near/to the vicinity of', جهرم the place-name, and رسید the past 'arrived/approached'.
segments.s594-g16: برآن دشت بی‌آب is a prepositional phrase 'upon that waterless plain' (برآن = 'on/that', دشت = 'plain', بی‌آب = 'waterless'). لشکر کشید is a verbal phrase 'he drew/placed/advanced an army' where کشید is the past of کشیدن ('to draw, to move/launch troops'); the verb here indicates mustering or deploying an army onto the plain.
segments.s594-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun.' برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'rose/appeared' (here 'came up'); سر 'head' = 'top/summit' is the thing the sun raises; از + تیغ کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the mountain's ridge/point.' Word order is poetic but follows 'when + subject + verb + object/pp'.
segments.s594-g58: This short nominal phrase lists those present: ردّان and بزرگان ایران as a group. The conjunction و links them; گروه is an appositive or summary noun 'a group/band.' The phrase is elliptical (no explicit verb here) and functions as the clause's subject or nominative continuation.
segments.s594-g59: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun phrase 'the reception / being received'; را is the definite-object marker pointing to that noun phrase; بیاراستند is past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural 'they arranged/prepared/laid out' (an archaic/poetic past form). The object precedes the verb here (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s594-g60: یکی modifies انجمن ('an assembly' or 'a single assembly'); دانشی is a genitive/attributive form 'of knowledge' (= 'learned' / 'of the learned'); خواستند is the past‑tense 3rd‑person plural 'they desired/summoned/asked for.' The order places modifiers before the head noun, and خواستند closes the clause.
segments.s594-g61: نهادند is past‑tense 3rd‑person plural 'they placed/put.' بهرام را is the marked direct object 'for Bahram' (را marks definite object/beneficiary). تخت عاج is the object noun phrase 'an ivory throne' (تخت = throne; عاج = ivory). The verb precedes its object in this poetic clause structure but functionally is 'they placed for Bahram an ivory throne.'
segments.s594-g62: This clause describes placing a crown on the head: به سر is a prepositional phrase 'on (his) head'; بر نهاده is a past participial/verb phrase 'having put/placed on' (برنهاد in poetic past); بهاگیر تاج is the noun phrase 'the Baha‑gir crown' (بهاگیر is an epithet/name modifying تاج). Word order is inverted/poetic: prepositional phrase + verb + crown phrase.
segments.s594-g63: نشستی here denotes 'a sitting/assembly/meeting' (a nominal use common in epic style). به آیین شاهنشهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/ceremony of emperors' (به = 'in/according to', آیین = 'rite/manner', شاهنشهان = 'emperors'). The whole phrase describes the type of meeting.
segments.s594-g64: بیاراست is used predicatively as 'well‑adorned / arranged' describing the sitting. کو is the relative/interrogative 'where' functioning here as 'in which/where'; بود is past‑tense 'was'; شاه جهان 'the king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان = world) is the subject that follows the verb in inverted poetic order (بود شاه جهان = 'the king of the world was there').
segments.s594-g25: سخن گوی is an imperative-like instruction built from the noun 'سخن' (speech) plus the verbal-form 'گوی' meaning 'say/utter' (classical brevity). The following و بشنو links another imperative 'بشنو' (listen); از ایشان سخن places 'سخن' as the verbal object or content and uses the prepositional phrase از ایشان ('from/of them') to mark source—literally 'listen to the speech from them'. Word order (verb(s) after the object phrase elements) is typical of older Persian poetic compression.
segments.s594-g26: کسی تیز گردد is a protasis (conditional/contingent clause) meaning 'if someone becomes sharp/angry/keen'; گرد(د) here is the classical third-person singular verbal form meaning 'becomes'. تو تیزی مکن is a negative imperative addressed to 'you' (تو) with مکن 'do not (be/make)', where تیزی (the noun 'sharpness') stands for the quality to be avoided—literally 'do not (show) sharpness'.
segments.s594-g27: بخوانیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'let us read/call/recite' (an inclusive proposal). تا introduces purpose/result ('so that/so as to') and چی(ست)شان contracts to چیستشان 'what they are', with در نهان 'in secret' as an adverbial phrase—together 'let us call so that we may know what they are in secret.'
segments.s594-g28: کرا is an archaic interrogative object meaning 'whom' placed fronted for emphasis; خواند خواهند is a split verbal cluster where خواهند (they will/desire) and خواند (to call/summon) combine—classical Persian allows this order—so the clause asks 'whom will the king of the world summon?' with شاه جهان (king of the world) as subject following the verb cluster.
segments.s594-g29: چو دانسته شد is a temporal passive-style clause meaning 'when it became/is known' (چو = when; دانسته past participle 'known'; شد auxiliary 'became/was'). چاره آن کنیم is the main clause: چاره 'remedy/measure', آن 'that', and کنیم (first-person-plural subjunctive/optative) 'we shall do/take'—together 'we will take that remedy.'
segments.s594-g30: گر introduces a conditional 'if'; آسان بود uses the copula بود in a counterfactual sense ('were easy'); the result clause کینه پنهان کنیم places the object کینه ('grudge') before the verb phrase پنهان کنیم ('we would hide'—پنهان as adjective/participle 'hidden' + کنیم 'we do' forming a periphrasis for 'hide'). The whole line reads as a counterfactual conditional: 'If it were easy, we would hide the grudge.'
segments.s594-g31: ور is a classical conditional 'if'; ایدون (a variant of آیند/ایَند) is third-person plural 'they come/appear'; کجا is the adverb 'where' or place marker (here functioning adverbially with the clause), and کین و جنگ آورند is the compound predicate 'they bring hatred and war' where آورند is third-person plural verbal form 'bring/produce'. The clause thus means 'If they come (and) bring enmity and war...'.
segments.s594-g32: بپیچند is a present/subjunctive third-person-plural verb 'they turn/they swerve/they twist' (here idiomatically 'they turn treacherously'); و simply coordinates; خوی پلنگ آورند uses خوی ('disposition/character') with the simile/epithet پلنگ ('leopard') as its modifier, and آورند 'they bring' figuratively means 'they assume/take on'—'they take on a leopard's nature.' Word order places the descriptive noun phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s594-g49: 'همین پادشاهی' = 'this very kingship' (emphatic 'همین' + noun). 'که' introduces a relative clause. 'میراث' is the head of that clause ('inheritance'), and 'تست' is an archaic/poetic contraction (تو + است) meaning 'is yours' — together: 'which is your inheritance'.
segments.s594-g50: Literal order: 'پدر بر پدر کرد' — past verb 'کرد' (he/she/it did/made) takes the preceding prepositional phrase. Here it conveys that the kingship was handed down 'from father to father' (the prepositional 'بر پدر' marking the target/position), and 'شاید درست' ('perhaps/possibly right') qualifies the whole statement. Note the terse, poetic omission of an explicit auxiliary for 'pass down'.
segments.s594-g51: 'سه دیگر' = 'three others/three more' (numeral + 'دیگر'). 'که' introduces a relative clause describing them. 'خون ریختن' is a verbal noun (masdar) meaning 'the shedding of blood' and functions as the subject/complement of 'کار ماست' — 'is our task/work'. 'ست' is the copula 'is'.
segments.s594-g52: 'همان' ('that same') picks out the previously mentioned group. 'ایزد دادگر' is a compound epithet: 'ایزد' = deity/God and 'دادگر' = just/righteous (an epithet). 'یار ماست' = 'is our ally' ('یار' = ally/companion, 'ماست' = 'is ours' with copula). The clause states God will be their ally.
segments.s594-g53: 'کسی را' (anyone as definite object) + 'جز از تو' ('except from you') together express exclusion: 'no one except you'. 'نخواهند' is 3rd person plural negative/future of 'خواستن' here meaning 'they will not want/desire' (i.e., they will desire no one). 'شاه' at the end identifies the role desired—'as king'—the predicate noun placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s594-g54: 'که' introduces the reason/explanation clause: '(for) that [you are] ...'. 'زیبای' is an adjectival form meaning 'beautiful'. 'تاجی' qualifies it ('of the crown' / 'crowned'), so 'beautiful when crowned' or 'beautiful with a crown'. The second conjunct 'و زیبای گاه' repeats the adjective ('and beautiful at the court/occasion/time'); the copula is omitted, a normal poetic compression.
segments.s594-g55: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the source ('from Munḏir'). 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. Word order places 'چو شاه' ('when the king') before the object 'این سخنها' ('these words'), and 'شنید' is the simple past 'he heard'. The clause thus reads 'when the king heard these words from Munḏir'.
segments.s594-g56: 'بخندید' is simple past 'he laughed'. 'و' = 'and'. 'شادان' ('joyful') modifies the following phrase; 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic 'ش' = his). 'بردمید' is a poetic past verb meaning 'was carried away' / 'leapt up' (figuratively: his heart was swept away with joy). Together: 'he laughed and, joyful, his heart leapt up.'
segments.s594-g17: سراپرده (sara-parde) is the noun 'inner pavilion / royal tent' and is the direct object of زد. زد is a past tense verb placed before its subject (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian); here زد means ‘set up / struck (a tent)’ in a pictorial/poetic sense. راد is an epithet 'the noble/generous' that directly modifies بهرامشاه (King Bahram) which is the subject following the verb.
segments.s594-g18: This clause places the verb آمد ('came') before the subject سپاه (the host/army) which appears at the end; به گرد اندر is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase meaning 'around/in a circle' and ز هر سو means 'from every side'. The whole unit reads 'troops came in around (him) from every side.' Note اندر is a classical/preclassical locative particle 'in/among'.
segments.s594-g19: به منذر is an indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to Munẓir'. چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; رای‌زن is the addressee's title 'counselor/adviser'. Word order places the addressee phrase after the reporting verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s594-g20: This is direct speech reported: به جهرم رسیدی is a second-person past/present-perfect sense 'you have arrived at / reached Jahrom' (رسیدی is 2nd singular). ز شهر یمن is the source phrase 'from the city of Yemen' with ز as the poetic 'from' and شهر یمن as a genitive compound 'the city of Yemen.' The subject 'you' is implied in the verb form.
segments.s594-g21: کنون جنگ سازیم is the clause 'now we shall make war' where سازیم is the 1st‑person‑plural future/optative 'we will make/do'. The following element گر is the conditional particle 'if' attached to this clause (written here fused: سازیم‌گر = سازیم + گر). گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'negotiation/discussion' and is the conditional complement ('if [there is] negotiation').
segments.s594-g22: چو = 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. به روی اندر آورد روی is a verbal phrase 'turned/ brought its face toward' (به روی...آوردن = to turn toward, اندر is a classical locative element 'in/among'), with the second روی the concrete noun 'face/front'. The clause describes the moment when the army turns its front/face toward the enemy/field.
segments.s594-g23: بدو = 'to him' (compound of be + 3rd‑person pronoun) indicating the addressee of گفت. منذر is the speaker's name. مهان را is the definite object 'the guests' marked by را. بخوان is an imperative 'call/invite' or 'summon' here (the verbal form is imperative/infinitival in context), so the whole clause is 'Munẓir said to him, "Call the guests".'
segments.s594-g24: چو آیند = 'when they come' (چو = when; آیند = 3rd‑person plural 'they come'). پیشت = 'before you / in your presence'. بیارای خوان is an imperative instruction: خوان = 'banquet/feast'; بیارای here functions as a verb 'prepare/arrange/ornament' (classical poetic usage) so the phrase means 'set the feast in order / make the banquet ready.'
segments.s594-g65: ز یک دست is an idiomatic locative 'from/on one side' (ز = poetic 'from/on'), with یک دست 'one hand/side' indicating location. The names بهرام (w2) and منذر (w3) follow that prepositional phrase as the logical subject. نشست is a past tense verb 'sat' (third-person past); in this poetic register the singular-form نشست is used to report the action for the (compound) subject that follows the locative phrase.
segments.s594-g66: دگر دست is the counterpart locative 'the other side' (دگر = 'other', دست = 'hand/side'). نعمان is the person on that side; و simply links the appositive phrase that follows. تیغی is an indefinite noun-form 'a sword' (here 'a blade'), and به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in (his) hand' specifying how he stood. Word order places the identifying noun (نعمان) before the descriptive phrase 'تیغی به دست'.
segments.s594-g67: همان here points back or emphasizes 'that very' assembly; گرد functions as 'assembly/around' (literally 'gathering' / 'around'), and بر گرد + noun is an idiom meaning 'around (the) ...'. پرده‌سرای is the place 'curtain-hall' or stage-house which is the object of the locative 'around'. The phrase order is demonstrative + noun + prepositional phrase (modifier) before the predicate.
segments.s594-g68: ستاده is a participial/predicative form 'standing' used as the main predicate describing the assembly. بزرگان تازی is a noun phrase 'the great ones — the Arabs' (بزرگان 'great ones/nobles' modified by the ethnic adjective تازی 'Arab'); the postpositional phrase به پای 'at the foot (of it)' gives the location relative to پرده‌سرا. Note the classical word order: place/description then the predicate (ستاده).
segments.s594-g69: از ایرانیان is a simple partitive/locative 'from among the Iranians'. آنک is a relative/introducing particle 'who/that' (introducing the person coming). بد پاک‌رای is a two-word descriptive phrase: پاک‌رای 'of pure/good reputation' is the core adjective, with بد here functioning in the poetic register as a modifier (intensifying or coloring the adjective in context). Together they describe the newcomer’s reputation.
segments.s594-g70: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به introduces the destination 'into/to' and takes دهلیز 'vestibule/corridor' as its object; پرده‌سرای specifies that it is the vestibule of the curtain-hall. The verb + به + place sequence marks motion into the named location.
segments.s594-g71: بفرمود is an honorific/formal past-tense verb 'he commanded/said (in an authoritative way)'. تا here introduces the result clause 'so that/and then' (classical usage: بفرمود تا = 'he commanded, so that...'). پرده is the direct object 'the curtain' and برداشتند is the past plural 'they removed/took up' (they performed the commanded action).
segments.s594-g72: ز درشان is a prepositional phrase 'from their door(s)' (ز = 'from', درشان = در + possessive -شان 'their door'). به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'with a voice' = 'aloud/with a shout' (به + noun used adverbially). بگذاشتند is the past plural 'they placed/put (it)' — here describing what they did with the curtain after taking it down.
segments.s594-g1: This is a compound subject: خود (emphatic 'himself') joined by و to شاه بهرام ('King Bahram'). با رای‌زن is a prepositional phrase 'with (the) counselors/advisers' that attaches to the subject, indicating who sat together.
segments.s594-g2: نشستند and گفتند are past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verbs agreeing with the compound subject; the conjunction و links the two actions. بی‌انجمن is an adverbial phrase 'without the assembly / apart from council', modifying گفتند (they spoke privately).
segments.s594-g3: سخنشان is سخن + شان 'their talk/speech'. بران راست شد is an idiomatic clause: بران ('to/on that') + راست شد ('was made/direct/turned (to)'); together 'their talk turned to that'. کز introduces the subordinate clause 'that/from which' and leads into the source phrase یمن ('Yemen').
segments.s594-g4: به ایران is the destination 'into Iran'. خرامند is a present/stative participial adjective 'strutting/striding proudly' describing the implied subjects from یمن; با انجمن 'with an assembly' is an accompanying/instrumental phrase describing how they came.
segments.s594-g5: گزین کرد is a compound past tense verb 'chose/selected' (گزین + کرد). از تازیان سی هزار is the object phrase 'thirty thousand from the Arabs' (numeric phrase follows the prepositional source).
segments.s594-g6: This nominative/predicative list describes those chosen: همه 'all' followed by two nominal compounds نیزه‌داران ('spear‑bearers') and خنجرگزار ('dagger‑bearers'). The copular verb ('were') is omitted but implied.
segments.s594-g7: به دینارشان marks the means/instrument 'with their dinars (their money)'. یکسر آباد کرد is a verbal phrase where یکسر 'entirely/wholly' qualifies آباد کرد 'made prosperous/made into flourishing (places)'; کرد is the past auxiliary.
segments.s594-g8: سر نامداران 'the heads (leaders) of the renowned' is the phrase receiving the action. پر از باد کرد is 'filled (پر) with (از) wind (باد)'; idiomatically here باد is figurative (literally 'wind') and the clause means 'filled their heads with wind', i.e. with pride or stirring emotion; کرد is the past verb.
segments.s594-g73: به here introduces the indirect object/recipient ('to/for') and precedes the title phrase 'شاه جهان‌آفرین' (a compound title: 'king — world‑creator'). خواندند is the past, 3rd person plural of خواندن used in epic register to mean they recited/sang aloud; the verb follows the indirect object and completes the clause.
segments.s594-g74: The prepositional phrase به مژگان marks the target/location ('on/at the eyelashes'); همی is the archaic/prosodic particle that gives a continuous or emphatic flavor to the past action, and برافشاندند is the past 3rd‑person plural of برافشاندن (prefix بر‑ + افشاندن) meaning ‘they showered/sprinkled/scattered’ (i.e., caused blood to fall). Word order is poetic: particle + object + verb.
segments.s594-g75: رسیدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of رسیدن meaning ‘they arrived/reached’. نزدیک is an adverb/adjectival complement meaning ‘near/close’, placed before the name to indicate proximity to بهرامشاه (Bahram the king), which is the goal/locative of the arrival.
segments.s594-g76: بدیدند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they saw’. The following phrase is inverted for poetic effect: زیبا (adjective) appears before یکی (a certain/one) and then the head noun تاج (crown); و گاه is a conjoined element (another object paired with تاج). The sequence therefore reports that they beheld a certain beautiful crown (and a companion item).
segments.s594-g77: به آواز گفتند is an idiom meaning ‘they said/cried aloud’ (به + noun 'آواز' marks the manner: 'in a voice/aloud'). گفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural ‘they said’ (here followed by the addressee). The following name phrase 'انوشه بدی' is vocative/recipient of the utterance; in the manuscript the second word looks like part of the name or a vocative particle and should be read together with the name.
segments.s594-g78: همیشه is an adverb meaning ‘always’. ز تو is a prepositional phrase ‘from you’ (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'), دور is an adjective 'distant/afar' modifying دست, which here functions idiomatically as 'hand/power/reach' ('far from your hand/reach'). بدی is the noun 'evil/harm' and is the semantic subject being said to be far. The phrase therefore forms a benedictory/wish: 'May harm always be far from you.'
segments.s594-g79: شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor'. پرسید is past 3rd‑person singular 'he asked'; و connects the two verbal actions. بنواختشان is past 3rd‑person singular of نواختن with the object pronoun ‑شان ('them') attached; here it means 'he caused them to sound/had them play' (i.e., ordered or set musicians to play or struck the instruments on their behalf).
segments.s594-g80: به اندازه is an adverbial phrase 'according to measure / to a proper degree'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', پایگه is the noun 'base/platform/station', and ساختشان is ساخت (past 3rd‑person singular) with the object pronoun ‑شان 'them' meaning 'he set/placed/made them' (built or positioned them upon the platform). The clause therefore says he placed them on the platform in proper measure/position.
segments.s594-g41: ور is the conditional 'if' introducing the clause. ایدونک is an archaic/deictic particle meaning roughly 'here/now' (poetic). گم‌کرده دارند is a periphrastic perfect-like construction: گم‌کرده (past-participle) + دارند (present auxiliary) = 'have lost' (literal word order is object after the compound). راه is the noun 'way/path' that they have lost.
segments.s594-g42: بخواهند is third-person plural present/subjunctive/optative meaning 'they will want' or 'they may wish'; it is followed by the infinitive بردن 'to lead/take'. همی is a frequentative/emphatic old particle marking continuity or habitual action ('still/continually' or emphatic), placed before the prepositional phrase. از تو is the preposition + pronoun 'from you' (i.e. 'away from you' or 'lead you away'), and گاه is an adverb 'at times/occasionally'.
segments.s594-g43: A coordinated subject: من 'I' + و + این سواران 'these horsemen' + و + شمشیر تیز 'the sharp sword'. The adjective تیز follows the noun (common in Persian) and modifies شمشیر. The demonstrative این specifies the riders.
segments.s594-g44: برانگیزم is first-person singular (suffix -م) of the verb برانگیختن/برانگیختن used optatively or as a future/performative 'I will rouse/raise'. اندر is an older preposition 'in/within', followed by جهان 'the world'. رستخیز is the noun 'uprising' or 'resurrection' (here contextually 'uprising'). The poetic word order places the verb before the locative phrase and its object.
segments.s594-g45: ببینی is a jussive/optative form 'may you see' (second person). بروهای is a plural form built on برو (here used as a noun: 'those who go' or 'champions/companions' in poetic usage) followed by the plural suffix -ها‌ی; پرچین is an adjective or epithet 'hedge/entrenchment, barrier' modifying بروهای, and من is the possessive pronoun 'my' placed after the noun in older/poetic Persian (i.e. 'my ...').
segments.s594-g46: فدای تو بادا is an optative formula: فدای 'sacrifice for/ devoted to', تو 'you', and بادا an archaic optative particle 'may it be'. Together 'may I (or may it) be devoted/sacrificed to you.' تن و دین من lists the things offered: تن 'body' and دین 'religion/faith' with the possessive pronoun من 'my' placed after them (poetic order).
segments.s594-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بینند is third-person plural present/subjunctive 'they see' or 'they will see' used as a general present. بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain adjective modifying سپاه (its exact meaning is obsolete in this edition but functions attributively). سپاه 'army' is the noun, and the following pieces (م + را) represent the older/poetic attachment of the first-person pronoun and/or object/topic marker (here indicating 'my/ of me' or marking the definite object), so سپاه مرا = 'my army'.
segments.s594-g48: همان is 'the same' or 'that very'; رسم 'custom/manner' and آیین 'rite/ceremony' are paired synonyms, joined by و, followed by راه 'way/method'. The trailing م (here part of the pronoun/enclitic) plus را again mark possession/definiteness (poetic order: '... way of mine' or 'my way'). The whole phrase states that they will observe the same custom, ritual, and way belonging to me.
segments.s594-g33: The clause begins with the subject/pronoun من (I) followed by the demonstrative این and the noun phrase دشتِ جهرم (the plain of Jahrom). چو here is the simile 'like/as' and دریا is the comparison target 'sea'. کنم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/optative from کردن used idiomatically for intention or future ('I will make/turn'). Word order is Subject — NP — simile — predicate, and the object of کنم (the plain) is understood from the earlier NP.
segments.s594-g34: ز is the poetic preposition 'by/with' (instrument/means). خورشید تابان is a noun + postpositive adjective 'the shining sun'. ثریا (the Pleiades) is the intended result-image, and کنم again 'I will make' (1st-person). The direct object (the plain) is omitted here and supplied from the prior clause; the clause means 'by the shining sun I will make it like the Pleiades.'
segments.s594-g35: بر آنم is a fixed idiom meaning 'I am intent/on that I am' = 'I intend'. که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/content. بینند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive ('may/should see' or 'shall see') used to express the intended outcome. چهرِ ترا is 'your face/appearance' with the 2nd-person suffix ت attached to چهر; the object-marking را is not placed here but appears later to cover the whole series of objects.
segments.s594-g36: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying what follows. برز و بالا are coordinated adjectives/nouns meaning 'exalted, lofty' (two near-synonyms joined). و مهرِ ت را continues the list with 'and your favour/affection' (مهر + 2nd-person suffix ت). The final را is the definite-object marker that applies to the whole coordinated noun phrase (it ties back to the verb بینند in the previous segment).
segments.s594-g37: A left-to-right list of qualities joined by و (and). خردمندی 'wisdom' is an abstract noun, رای here means 'counsel/plan' (sense: judgment/advice), فرهنگ 'culture/refinement'. The final تو is the possessive 'your' applied to the entire series (postposed possessive common in poetry).
segments.s594-g38: Another coordinated list: شکیبایی 'patience', دانش 'knowledge/learning', and سنگ 'stone' used figuratively for firmness/steadfastness. As in the previous segment, the final تو is the possessive 'your' that governs the whole list. The nouns are simple coordinations describing personal qualities.
segments.s594-g39: نخواهند is 3rd-person plural negative-future/optative of خواستن ('they will not want' / 'will not desire'); the phrase جز تو کسی ('except you anyone') is inverted for emphasis and means 'no one but you'. تخت را is the definite object 'the throne' marked by را. Together the word order yields 'no one except you will want the throne.'
segments.s594-g40: کله را 'the head' (here: crown/summit) with را marks it as another definite object; و joins the next phrase. زیبای...بخت را represents زیباییِ بخت 'the beauty of fortune' — in the manuscript the ezafe/linking vowel is not fully written, so زیبای + ی + بخت shows that relation. The final را again marks the definite object; overall the coordinated objects are 'the throne, the crown, and the beauty of fortune.'

sentences.s594-s5: When this news reached Iran, a young man came before the brave ones.
sentences.s594-s6: The great men became sorrowful because of that affair; they went to Azarpak Barzin.
sentences.s594-s7: They prayed to God, asking that the fighting might turn back to joy and feasting.
sentences.s594-s8: When Mundhir came near Jahrom, he drew up his army on that waterless plain.
sentences.s594-s29: When the sun lifted its head from the mountain's ridge, the riders and the great men of Iran formed a band.
sentences.s594-s30: They prepared the reception; they summoned an assembly of learned men.
sentences.s594-s31: They set an ivory throne for Bahram and placed the Baha‑gir crown upon his head.
sentences.s594-s32: They arranged a sitting in the imperial manner, where the king of the world was present.
sentences.s594-s13: Speak, and listen to their words; if anyone becomes sharp, do not be sharp yourself.
sentences.s594-s14: Let us call (or read) so that we may know what they are in secret; whom will the king of the world summon?
sentences.s594-s15: When it is known, we will take that remedy; if it were easy, we would hide the grudge.
sentences.s594-s16: But if they come and bring hatred and war, they will swerve aside and assume the nature of a leopard.
sentences.s594-s25: This very kingship, which is your inheritance, passed from father to father — perhaps rightly so.
sentences.s594-s26: Three others, for whom shedding blood is our work; that same just God is our ally.
sentences.s594-s27: They will want no one as king except you, for you are beautiful with a crown and beautiful in presence.
sentences.s594-s28: When the king heard these words from Munḏir, he laughed, and his heart leapt for joy.
sentences.s594-s9: The noble Bahram-shah raised his inner pavilion; troops came in around him from every side.
sentences.s594-s10: He said to Munẓir thus: 'O counselor, you have come to Jahrom from the city of Yemen.'
sentences.s594-s11: Now we will make war; if there is any negotiation — when the host turns its face to the front —
sentences.s594-s12: Munẓir said to him, 'Call the guests; when they come before you, set the banquet in order.'
sentences.s594-s33: On one side sat Bahram and Munḏir; on the other side Nuʿmān, a sword in hand.
sentences.s594-s34: Around the curtain-hall stood the assembly — the great Arab nobles at its foot.
sentences.s594-s35: From among the Iranians there came one of pure repute into the vestibule of the curtain-hall.
sentences.s594-s36: He ordered, and they removed the curtain; from their doors they, with a shout, set it (aside).
sentences.s594-s1: King Bahram himself sat with the counselors, and they spoke apart from the assembly.
sentences.s594-s2: Their talk turned to that: that from Yemen they were swaggering into Iran with an assembly.
sentences.s594-s3: He selected thirty thousand from the Arabs, all spear‑bearers and dagger‑wielders.
sentences.s594-s4: With their dinars he entirely made (things) prosperous, and filled the heads of the renowned with wind.
sentences.s594-s37: They sang to the King, the Creator of the World; they showered blood upon their eyelashes.
sentences.s594-s38: They came close to Bahramshah and saw a beautiful crown and diadem.
sentences.s594-s39: They cried aloud to Anoushe (calling his name), 'May evil always be far from you.'
sentences.s594-s40: The Shahanshah asked and had them play; he placed them upon the platform in due measure.
sentences.s594-s21: If those who have lost the way here should wish to lead you off at times.
sentences.s594-s22: I and these horsemen and the sharp sword will raise an uprising in the world.
sentences.s594-s23: May you behold my hedge-like champions; may my body and my faith be sacrificed for you.
sentences.s594-s24: When they see my army, they will find the same custom, ritual, and way of mine.
sentences.s594-s17: I will turn this plain of Jahrom into a sea; by the shining sun I will make it (as) the Pleiades.
sentences.s594-s18: I intend that they should see your face thus — your loftiness and exaltation and your favour.
sentences.s594-s19: Your wisdom, counsel, and culture; your patience, learning, and steadfastness.
sentences.s594-s20: No one except you will desire the throne, the crown, and the beauty of fortune.
